Mojave Nature by Kurt Buzard


Antirrhinum mohavea

Golden Lesser Mohavea

A charming desert annual with pale yellow, snapdragon-like flowers that exhibit a fascinating evolutionary mimicry of Mentzelia blossoms. Antirrhinum mohavea is a specialist of the Mojave Desert. It is smaller than its cousin, M. confertiflora, and lacks the dramatic purple centers. Interestingly, this plant does not produce nectar; it attracts bees by mimicking the appearance of Mentzelia flowers, which bloom in the same habitat and do provide a reward, a strategy known as floral mimicry.

Learn More Details About This Plant

Plant Data

Names and Taxonomy

  • Common Names: golden lesser mohavea, lesser mohavea
  • Synonyms: antirrhinum breviflorum, Mohavea breviflora
  • Family: plantaginaceae
  • Genus Information: CA 2, NV 2, AZ 2

Habit and Size

  • Duration: annual herb
  • Habit: erect to ascending
  • Shape: branched / upright
  • Size: 5–20 cm tall
  • Stem: glandular-pubescent (viscid)

Leaves

  • Color: light green
  • Shape: lanceolate to ovate
  • Edge: entire
  • Arrangement: alternate (lower leaves sometimes opposite)
  • Leaf Retention: annual

Flowers and Fruit

  • Sex: hermaphroditic
  • Flower Color: lemon yellow with purple speckling in the throat
  • Flower Size: 1.5–2 cm long
  • Number of Petals: 5 (fused into a two-lipped tube)
  • Bloom: February–April
  • Fruit: ovoid capsule

Habitat and Growing Conditions

  • Water Use: low
  • Light: full sun
  • Soil: sandy or gravelly washes and slopes
  • Habitat: creosote bush scrub, desert washes

Distribution

  • Distribution: CA, NV, AZ
  • Seen: Ubehebe Crater, Death Valley
  • Elevation: -200–3500 feet (-60–1065 meters)
